chunk_summary: This section discusses inpatient stabilization and antibiotic regimens for children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) complicated by stunting, focusing on triage, treatment protocols, and antibiotic use to manage infections and complications.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of antibiotics in managing complicated SAM with stunting.
Learn the triage and stabilization priorities for children with complicated SAM.
Identify appropriate antibiotic regimens and their dosages for inpatient care.
Recognize the importance of reassessment and documentation in SAM management pathways.
key_facts: Children with SAM often have impaired immune responses and may present with serious bacterial infections without classic signs.
Empiric parenteral antibiotic regimen: ampicillin 50 mg/kg every 6 hours plus gentamicin 7.5 mg/kg once daily.
Step down to oral amoxicillin 25–40 mg/kg every 8 hours once the child stabilizes and can take oral feeds.
Persistent fever should prompt evaluation for pneumonia, urinary infection, malaria, or TB rather than repeated empiric antibiotics.
Documenting the appetite test result and antibiotic start/stop dates is critical in SAM management.
reference_quote: Children with SAM have impaired immune responses and may have serious bacterial infection without classic signs, so antibiotics are part of stabilization when illness is complicated. (Source: WHO ELENA, https://www.who.int/tools/elena/interventions/antibiotics-sam)
document_key_topics: Triage and management of complications in SAM
Documentation and reassessment in SAM pathways
